2026-W28.summary: 1X gave its Neo humanoid near-human hands and Mitsubishi joined the android race with a 2027 factory plan, while Waymo pushed driverless rides into a half-dozen new cities and Ondas snapped up drone maker DZYNE for $875.8 million.
2026-W28.lede: The humanoids arrived in force this week, some promising companionship and others promising to replace factory shifts, though nearly every founder spent as much energy managing expectations as raising them. Meanwhile the machines that already work for a living—Waymo's robotaxis and a fleet of autonomous drones from Everest to Tampa Bay—quietly expanded, a reminder that the future often shows up as logistics rather than spectacle.
2026-W28.linkedin:
This week the robots kept their promises modest and their expansion plans large.

→ 1X gave its Neo humanoid hands with something close to human-level dexterity and touch sensitivity — the unglamorous work behind any "companion robot" pitch.

→ Mitsubishi joined the android race outright, targeting 2027 to manufacture and deploy humanoid workers on factory floors.

→ Waymo pushed driverless rides into Denver, San Diego, Tampa, and beyond — proof that the driverless future arrives as a rollout schedule, not a headline.

→ Ondas bought military drone maker DZYNE for $875.8 million, while Skydio quietly crossed 1,000 autonomous dock deployments in a single year.

The pattern holds: humanoids get the press releases, but the machines already earning their keep — robotaxis, delivery drones, inspection fleets — are the ones actually rewriting logistics, one unremarkable trip at a time.

The full edition is on vertebrate.ai.

#Robotics #Humanoids #PhysicalAI #Driverless #Drones #AI

2026-W29.summary: Figure unveiled its third-generation humanoid, Figure 03, alongside a Helix upgrade that lets robots learn new chores straight from video; NEURA Robotics struck a major chip-and-software alliance with Qualcomm to standardize humanoid 'brains'; and Anduril's autonomous drone fired its first live missile, quietly formalizing a new class of lethal, uncrewed weapons.
2026-W29.lede: This week the humanoid race shifted from demo-day flourish to something closer to infrastructure. Figure, NEURA Robotics, and Dexterity all made moves toward robots that generalize and scale, while GeniusRobotics claimed a manufacturing first and Anduril's missile test reminded everyone that autonomy's hardest edges are still military ones.
2026-W29.linkedin:
Robots got smarter, chattier, and — in one case — armed. 🤖

→ Figure unveiled Figure 03, its third-gen humanoid built for factory-scale manufacturing and trained on its proprietary Helix AI.
→ NEURA Robotics and Qualcomm are teaming up on a 'Brain + Nervous System' architecture to standardize humanoid hardware and software.
→ Dexterity introduced Foresight, a world model trained on 100 million real warehouse actions so robots can predict outcomes before acting.
→ Anduril's autonomous drone fired its first live missile, a milestone that formalizes lethal, uncrewed weapons as a category, not a concept.

Meanwhile GeniusRobotics claims a mass-production first in China, Tesla's Robotaxi quietly expanded in Miami, and drone delivery keeps creeping into daily life — from Papa Johns pizza to EPA-approved methane sniffing.

The full edition is on vertebrate.ai.
